subject: criminal_law
chapter_title: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
topic_heading: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
fact_pattern: Roland was convicted of second degree murder in 1998 and has exhausted all conventional appeals, including an unsuccessful application for leave to the Supreme Court of Canada. New DNA evidence has emerged that Roland's supporters argue conclusively exonerates him. Roland's counsel wishes to pursue any available remedy.
call_of_question: Under s. 696.1 of the Criminal Code, what process is available to Roland, and what must be established to obtain a remedy?

options.A: Roland may apply directly to the Supreme Court of Canada for a re-hearing of his appeal on the basis of the new evidence.
options.B: Roland may apply to the Minister of Justice under s. 696.1 for a review of his conviction; the Minister may direct a new trial or refer the matter to the Court of Appeal if satisfied there is a reasonable basis to conclude a miscarriage of justice likely occurred.
options.C: Roland may apply to the Ontario Court of Appeal for a fresh evidence hearing under s. 683 of the Criminal Code without any preconditions.
options.D: Roland's only remedy is a pardon or record suspension through the Parole Board of Canada.

explanation: Under s.696.1 of the Criminal Code establishes the ministerial review mechanism — sometimes called the 'conviction review' process — for persons who have been convicted of an offence and who have exhausted all available appeals. The applicant must satisfy the Minister of Justice that there are reasonable grounds to conclude that a miscarriage of justice likely occurred. If satisfied, under s. 696.3, the Minister may: (a) direct a new trial before any court the Minister thinks proper; or (b) refer the matter to the Court of Appeal for hearing and determination as if it were an appeal by the convicted person (or on a specific question as if it were a question of law). The ministerial review process is an extraordinary remedy of last resort designed to address wrongful convictions — it is available only after conventional appeals are exhausted. The DNA evidence in Roland's case would be the basis for the application, and the Minister would assess whether it provides reasonable grounds to conclude a miscarriage of justice likely occurred.
why_A_wrong: The Supreme Court of Canada does not accept applications for re-hearing based on new evidence after leave has been denied. The SCC has no mechanism to revisit concluded appeals. The extraordinary remedy for wrongful convictions is the ministerial review under s.696.1, not a further appeal.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 696.1 provides the ministerial review mechanism. After exhausting all appeals, Roland may apply to the Minister of Justice, who may direct a new trial or refer to the Court of Appeal if satisfied there are reasonable grounds to conclude a miscarriage of justice likely occurred.
why_C_wrong: A fresh evidence application to the Court of Appeal under s.683 is not available independently once all appeals have been exhausted. The s.683 powers are ancillary to a pending appeal — they do not create a stand-alone avenue to re-open a conviction after all appeals are concluded. The ministerial review under s.696.1 is the pathway.
why_D_wrong: A pardon or record suspension through the Parole Board of Canada does not address the underlying conviction — it merely suspends the criminal record from the national repository. A pardon does not constitute an exoneration or legally overturn the conviction. Roland's goal of exoneration requires the ministerial review process, not a pardon.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
source_reference.heading: Ministerial Review — s. 696.1
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 696.1, s. 696.3

tested_concepts: s.696.1 ministerial review — last resort remedy
reasonable grounds to conclude miscarriage of justice likely occurred
Minister may direct new trial or refer to Court of Appeal
available only after all appeals exhausted

subject: criminal_law
chapter_title: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
topic_heading: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
subtopic_heading: Miscarriage of Justice — Standard on Conviction Appeal — s. 686(1)(a)(iii)
fact_pattern: The accused was convicted of manslaughter. On appeal, the Court of Appeal finds that defence counsel at trial failed to call a critical alibi witness whose evidence was known to counsel before trial. The defence of the case at trial was inadequate. The accused argues his conviction resulted in a miscarriage of justice.
call_of_question: Under s. 686(1)(a)(iii) of the Criminal Code, what must the accused demonstrate to establish that the conviction resulted in a miscarriage of justice?

options.A: The accused must prove that defence counsel was incompetent and that this incompetence caused the conviction.
options.B: The accused must show that a miscarriage of justice occurred — including that the trial was unfair or the verdict is unreliable as a result of counsel's failure — such that the verdict cannot be allowed to stand.
options.C: The accused need only show that the outcome might have been different if the alibi witness had testified.
options.D: The accused must demonstrate that the trial judge was aware of the ineffective assistance and failed to intervene.

explanation: Under s.686(1)(a)(iii) allows the Court of Appeal to allow an appeal where the verdict resulted in a miscarriage of justice. For claims of ineffective assistance of counsel, the test is derived from R v GDB, 2000 SCC 22: the accused must establish (1) that counsel's acts or omissions constituted incompetence (falling below the standard of a reasonably competent criminal lawyer), and (2) that the incompetence resulted in a miscarriage of justice — meaning the trial was not fair or the verdict is unreliable. The miscarriage of justice component requires more than showing the outcome 'might have been' different — it requires a reasonable probability that the verdict would have been different but for the incompetence, or that the trial was rendered fundamentally unfair. Merely proving incompetence without the resulting prejudice is insufficient.
why_A_wrong: Proving incompetence alone is not sufficient under the GDB test. The accused must also establish the prejudice component — that the incompetence resulted in a miscarriage of justice (unreliable verdict or unfair trial). The two-part test requires both incompetence and resulting prejudice.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s.686(1)(a)(iii) and the GDB test, the accused must show: (1) counsel's acts were incompetent, and (2) the incompetence resulted in a miscarriage of justice (unfair trial or unreliable verdict). Both components are required.
why_C_wrong: The 'might have been different' standard is too low. The test requires a reasonable probability that the result would have been different — not merely a possibility. If the outcome might conceivably have been different, that does not establish the miscarriage of justice required. The threshold is more demanding than mere possibility.
why_D_wrong: There is no requirement that the trial judge was aware of and failed to address the ineffective assistance. The trial judge is not responsible for monitoring the quality of defence counsel's advocacy. The analysis on appeal focuses on counsel's conduct and its impact on the verdict, not on whether the judge intervened.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
source_reference.heading: Miscarriage of Justice — Ineffective Assistance of Counsel — s. 686(1)(a)(iii)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 686(1)(a)(iii); R v GDB, 2000 SCC 22

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: ineffective assistance of counsel — failure to call material witness known before trial

tested_concepts: s.686(1)(a)(iii) miscarriage of justice ground
GDB two-part test: incompetence + prejudice
unreliable verdict vs unfair trial
reasonable probability standard for prejudice
ineffective assistance of counsel on appeal

subject: criminal_law
chapter_title: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
topic_heading: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
subtopic_heading: Stay of Proceedings as Extraordinary Remedy — Charter s. 24(1)
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with drug trafficking. During the investigation, police engaged in outrageous misconduct: they planted evidence, fabricated documents, and suborned perjury from informants. The accused moves under s. 24(1) of the Charter for a stay of proceedings, arguing that proceeding to trial would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
call_of_question: Under the framework established in R v O'Connor (1995) and affirmed in subsequent jurisprudence, what is the legal test for granting a stay of proceedings as a remedy under s. 24(1) of the Charter?

options.A: A stay is available whenever the accused's Charter rights have been violated, regardless of the severity of the breach.
options.B: A stay is available only for breaches of ss. 7, 11(d), or 11(h) of the Charter — not for all Charter rights.
options.C: A stay is an exceptional remedy granted only where: (1) the prejudice to the accused's right to a fair trial or to the integrity of the justice system will be manifested, perpetuated, or aggravated through the conduct of the trial or its outcome; and (2) no other remedy is reasonably capable of removing that prejudice.
options.D: A stay is an available remedy wherever a court finds that police misconduct occurred, as a general deterrent.

explanation: Under Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(1), In R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, the Supreme Court established the two-part test for granting a stay of proceedings under s. 24(1) of the Charter as an abuse of process or Charter remedy. The test is: (1) the prejudice to the accused's right to a fair trial or to the integrity of the justice system will be manifested, perpetuated, or aggravated through the conduct of the trial or by its outcome; and (2) no other remedy is reasonably capable of removing that prejudice. The stay is described as a remedy of last resort — granted only in the 'clearest of cases' where irreparable harm to the fairness of the trial or to the integrity of the justice system cannot otherwise be addressed. On these facts (planted evidence, fabricated documents, suborned perjury), the case is a strong candidate for a stay as the only remedy that would address the systemic abuse — proceeding to trial would perpetuate the prejudice to the justice system's integrity.
why_A_wrong: A stay is not available for every Charter violation. It is an exceptional, last-resort remedy. For routine Charter breaches, s.24(2) exclusion of evidence or other remedies are more appropriate. The O'Connor test confines stays to the clearest of cases — the remedy is not triggered by every Charter violation.
why_B_wrong: The availability of a stay is not restricted to specific Charter sections. A stay may be granted for serious breaches of any Charter right (or for abuse of process at common law), provided the O'Connor two-part test is satisfied. There is no closed list of Charter sections that ground a stay.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The O'Connor two-part test requires: (1) prejudice to trial fairness or system integrity manifested/perpetuated/aggravated by continuing; and (2) no other remedy capable of removing the prejudice. This is the established framework for stays under s.24(1).
why_D_wrong: General deterrence of police misconduct is not a sufficient basis for a stay. The s.24(1) remedy must be proportionate to the actual prejudice — a stay based solely on deterrence without satisfying the O'Connor test would be an error of law. Deterrence may inform the analysis but cannot substitute for the two-part test.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
source_reference.heading: Stay of Proceedings — Charter s. 24(1) — O'Connor Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 24(1); R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411

tested_concepts: O'Connor two-part test for stay of proceedings
stay as last-resort remedy
prejudice to fair trial or justice system integrity
no other remedy capable of removing prejudice
clearest of cases standard

subject: criminal_law
chapter_title: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
topic_heading: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
subtopic_heading: Entrapment — R v Mack — Stay as Exclusive Remedy
fact_pattern: Police suspected Marcus of drug dealing but had no evidence. An undercover officer persistently contacted Marcus for several weeks, each time requesting that Marcus obtain cocaine for the officer. Marcus initially refused repeatedly but eventually agreed and provided cocaine to the undercover officer. Marcus is charged with trafficking. He raises the defence of entrapment.
call_of_question: Under the entrapment doctrine established in R v Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903, which of the following correctly describes the legal framework and the available remedy if entrapment is established?

options.A: Entrapment is a complete defence that, if proven, results in an acquittal because it negates the accused's criminal intent.
options.B: Entrapment constitutes an abuse of process; if established, the court must enter a stay of proceedings — this is the only available remedy.
options.C: Entrapment is available only where police had absolutely no reasonable suspicion of the accused before initiating contact.
options.D: Entrapment negates the actus reus of the offence because the illegal act was induced by state agents, resulting in an acquittal.

explanation: Under R v Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903, In R v Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903, the Supreme Court held that entrapment in Canada is not a substantive defence that negates mens rea or actus reus, but is instead an abuse of process doctrine. If entrapment is established, the court must enter a stay of proceedings — not an acquittal. Two forms of entrapment were recognized: (1) opportunity-based entrapment, where the state provides an opportunity to commit a crime without reasonable suspicion or bona fide inquiry that the person is involved in criminal activity; and (2) inducement-based entrapment (conduct-based), where the state goes beyond providing an opportunity and actually induces or persuades a person who would not otherwise have committed the offence. On these facts, Marcus's repeated refusals followed by the officer's persistent approach suggests conduct-based entrapment. The remedy for entrapment, if established, is exclusively a stay of proceedings — not an acquittal — because the abuse of process analysis is directed at the conduct of the state, not the guilt of the accused.
why_A_wrong: Entrapment in Canada does not result in an acquittal. It is an abuse of process doctrine, not a substantive defence negating mens rea or actus reus. An acquittal is not the appropriate remedy — a stay of proceedings is the exclusive remedy for entrapment.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Mack, entrapment is an abuse of process doctrine. If established, the exclusive remedy is a stay of proceedings — not an acquittal. The stay is entered because state conduct, not the accused's guilt, is the focus.
why_C_wrong: The requirement is not that police had 'absolutely no reasonable suspicion.' The Mack test asks whether police were acting on reasonable suspicion or as part of a bona fide inquiry, or whether they went beyond providing an opportunity to actively inducing the offence. Entrapment can be found even where police had some prior suspicion if their conduct crossed the line into impermissible inducement.
why_D_wrong: Entrapment does not negate the actus reus. The accused committed the physical act — providing cocaine is trafficking regardless of police inducement. Entrapment focuses on the propriety of the state's conduct, not on whether the elements of the offence were met. The remedy is a stay, not an acquittal.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
source_reference.heading: Entrapment — Abuse of Process — Stay of Proceedings
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R v Mack, [1988] 2 SCR 903; Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46

tested_concepts: entrapment as abuse of process in Canada
stay of proceedings as exclusive remedy for entrapment
opportunity-based vs conduct-based entrapment (Mack)
entrapment does not result in acquittal

subject: criminal_law
chapter_title: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
topic_heading: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
subtopic_heading: Lost or Destroyed Evidence — Miscarriage of Justice and Appropriate Remedy
fact_pattern: During a murder investigation, police collected biological samples from a crime scene and had them analyzed. After the analysis, the samples were lost before trial, making retesting impossible. The accused is convicted at trial. On appeal, the accused argues the lost evidence resulted in a miscarriage of justice because defence experts could not independently test the samples.
call_of_question: Where evidence is lost or destroyed by the Crown, which of the following best describes the framework courts apply to determine whether a miscarriage of justice occurred and what remedy, if any, is appropriate?

options.A: Lost evidence automatically entitles the accused to a stay of proceedings because any Crown destruction of evidence is a per se Charter breach under s. 7.
options.B: The court assesses whether the loss of evidence was the result of bad faith or negligence, whether the evidence was material to the accused's ability to make full answer and defence, and whether prejudice to the accused can be remedied — with a stay available in only the clearest of cases.
options.C: Lost evidence creates a presumption of innocence in the accused's favour that the Crown must rebut, and the trial is automatically invalidated.
options.D: The loss of physical evidence is irrelevant if the Crown still has expert reports summarizing the analysis — the right to full answer and defence is satisfied by disclosure of the reports.

explanation: Under Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7, Where the Crown loses or destroys evidence, the court applies a contextual framework: (1) Was the loss the result of bad faith, unacceptable negligence, or something less? (2) Was the evidence material — would it have been useful to the defence? (3) Can the resulting prejudice be remedied by a lesser measure? The Supreme Court in R v La, [1997] 2 SCR 680, and R v Bero (2000) ONCA confirmed that the loss of evidence can constitute a breach of s. 7 (right to make full answer and defence), but the appropriate remedy depends on the circumstances. Automatic stays are not granted — a stay is reserved for the clearest of cases where the prejudice cannot otherwise be remedied and a fair trial is impossible. Bad faith in destruction of evidence is more likely to warrant a stay than inadvertent loss. Mere negligence resulting in lost evidence typically warrants a remedy short of a stay.
why_A_wrong: Lost evidence does not automatically warrant a stay of proceedings. The court must examine the circumstances of the loss, whether it was bad faith or negligence, and whether lesser remedies can address the prejudice. A stay is an exceptional remedy of last resort, not an automatic consequence of lost evidence.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Courts assess the circumstances of the loss (bad faith vs. negligence), the materiality of the evidence, and whether the prejudice can be remedied. A stay is available in only the clearest of cases where no lesser remedy suffices.
why_C_wrong: Loss of evidence does not create a presumption of innocence or automatically invalidate the trial. The accused bears the burden of showing that the loss constituted a breach of s. 7 and caused prejudice to the right to make full answer and defence. There is no 'automatic invalidation' of a trial based on lost evidence.
why_D_wrong: While disclosure of expert reports mitigates some prejudice, it may not fully address the loss of the right to independent testing. Where biological samples are lost and independent retesting would have been material to the defence, the right to full answer and defence may still be compromised even if written reports were disclosed. The court must assess actual prejudice to the defence.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
source_reference.heading: Lost or Destroyed Evidence — s. 7 Charter and Remedies
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7; R v La, [1997] 2 SCR 680

tested_concepts: framework for lost/destroyed evidence: bad faith, materiality, remedy
stay not automatic for lost evidence
bad faith destruction vs. negligent loss
full answer and defence under s. 7 Charter

subject: criminal_law
chapter_title: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
topic_heading: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
subtopic_heading: Abuse of Process — Common Law Doctrine vs. Charter s. 7
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with assault. Before the preliminary inquiry, the Crown attorney made a public statement to the media suggesting that the accused is guilty and describing in detail evidence not yet disclosed. Defence counsel brings a motion to stay the proceedings, arguing that the Crown's media statement constitutes an abuse of process that has irreparably tainted the jury pool and undermined the fairness of the proceeding.
call_of_question: Where the abuse of process alleged does not involve a specific Charter violation but rather prosecutorial conduct that undermines the integrity of the process, which of the following correctly describes the available framework?

options.A: The court has no power to grant a stay unless the accused identifies a specific Charter right that was violated — common law abuse of process was subsumed into the Charter after 1982.
options.B: The abuse of process doctrine exists independently at common law and under s. 7 of the Charter; a stay may be granted for conduct that undermines the integrity of the justice system even if no specific enumerated Charter right was breached.
options.C: Abuse of process is an exclusively pre-Charter doctrine and cannot be applied in a modern proceeding governed by the Charter.
options.D: The remedy for abuse of process based on prosecutorial conduct is always a permanent stay — courts have no discretion to impose lesser remedies.

explanation: Under Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7, the abuse of process doctrine has both a common law foundation and a Charter s. 7 dimension. Prior to the Charter, courts recognized that proceedings could be stayed where the Crown's conduct was so unfair or oppressive as to amount to an abuse of process — even without a specific constitutional violation. The Charter did not replace this doctrine; rather, s. 7 of the Charter (the right to life, liberty, and security of the person, including the right to a fair trial) provides an additional constitutional basis for challenging prosecutorial misconduct. In R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411 and subsequent cases, the Supreme Court confirmed that a stay remains an exceptional remedy available in the 'clearest of cases' where: (1) the prejudice to the accused's right to a fair trial or to the integrity of the justice system will be manifested, perpetuated, or aggravated through the conduct of the trial or its outcome; and (2) no other remedy is reasonably capable of removing that prejudice. Courts retain discretion to grant lesser remedies (e.g., a jury instruction, exclusion of evidence) before resorting to a stay.
why_A_wrong: The common law abuse of process doctrine was not subsumed into or replaced by the Charter. It continues to operate independently. Courts can grant stays under the common law doctrine without requiring the accused to establish a specific enumerated Charter breach. Both streams coexist.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Abuse of process operates at common law and under Charter s. 7. A stay may be granted for conduct that undermines the integrity of the justice system without requiring a violation of a specific enumerated Charter right, provided the O'Connor test is satisfied.
why_C_wrong: Abuse of process is not an exclusively pre-Charter doctrine. The Supreme Court has consistently applied and developed it post-Charter, expressly holding that the doctrine continues to operate both under the common law and as part of Charter s. 7 jurisprudence.
why_D_wrong: The remedy for abuse of process is not automatically a permanent stay. Courts have a range of remedies available and choose the one appropriate to the circumstances. A stay is reserved for the clearest of cases — courts may instead give directions to the jury, order production of materials, or provide other remedies short of a stay. The O'Connor test contemplates that a stay is appropriate only where no lesser remedy can remove the prejudice.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 12: Miscarriage of Justice and Extraordinary Remedies
source_reference.heading: Abuse of Process — Common Law and Charter s. 7
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7; R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411

tested_concepts: abuse of process doctrine — common law and Charter s. 7
stay available without specific Charter violation
O'Connor test applies to common law abuse of process
discretion to grant lesser remedies — stay not automatic
